---
title: "Teaching an RC car to drive itself: DonkeyCar on a Jetson Nano"
subtitle: "A weekend self-driving project, and what it taught me about data over models"
description: >
  Building a DonkeyCar on a Jetson Nano — the hardware, the data-collection loop,
  training a behavioural-cloning model on the edge, and why the dataset (not the
  architecture) decides whether it makes the corner.
date: 2026-06-26
categories: [robotics, edge ml, jetson, side project]
image: donkeycar.jpg          # a photo of the build
image-alt: "DonkeyCar with a Jetson Nano"
draft: true   # <- remove this line to publish
---

> **Outline / draft.** Technical skeleton with a runnable data-inspection cell.
> Fill the prose with your own build notes.

## The build

> The hardware list and what each part does. Keep it skimmable:

| Part | Role |
|------|------|
| Jetson Nano (4 GB) | On-board inference + training |
| RC car chassis + ESC | The actual vehicle |
| Wide-angle CSI camera | The only sensor the model gets |
| PCA9685 (PWM driver) | Steering + throttle control |
| Power bank / battery | Untethered runtime |

> A paragraph on the setup pain points (JetPack version, the SD-card image,
> getting the camera recognised) — the stuff the official docs gloss over.

## The DonkeyCar loop

The whole workflow is three commands. First, scaffold the car:

```bash
donkey createcar --path ~/mycar
```

Then drive it manually to collect labelled data — each frame is saved with the
steering and throttle you commanded (this is behavioural cloning):

```bash
python ~/mycar/manage.py drive   # records "tubs" of (image, steering, throttle)
```

Then train an autopilot and hand control back to it:

```bash
donkey train --tub ~/mycar/data --model ~/mycar/models/pilot.h5
python ~/mycar/manage.py drive --model ~/mycar/models/pilot.h5
```

## Look at your data before you train anything

The single biggest lever isn't the model — it's whether your driving data is
*balanced*. Drive the track only clockwise and the model learns "always turn
left." Here's a quick look at the steering distribution in a tub:

```{python}
#| label: fig-steering-balance
#| fig-cap: "Steering-angle distribution across a recorded tub. A spike at 0 (straights) and a left/right imbalance both predict exactly where the car will fail."
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show the tub-inspection code"
#| eval: false   # set to true locally once TUB_DIR points at real data
import json, glob, os
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

TUB_DIR = os.path.expanduser("~/mycar/data")  # <- your tub
records = []
for f in glob.glob(os.path.join(TUB_DIR, "**", "record_*.json"), recursive=True):
    with open(f) as fh:
        records.append(json.load(fh))

steering = np.array([r["user/angle"] for r in records])  # in [-1, 1]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3))
ax.hist(steering, bins=41, color="#0b5e54")
ax.axvline(0, color="#c0392b", ls="--", lw=1)
ax.set_xlabel("steering angle"); ax.set_ylabel("frames")
ax.set_title(f"{len(steering)} frames — mean {steering.mean():+.3f}")
fig.tight_layout()
```

> Then talk about the fixes you actually used: driving both directions,
> down-sampling the zero-steering spike, augmentation. This is the part where
> your day-job instincts (data curation, class balance) transfer directly.

## Squeezing it onto the Nano

> The edge-ML angle that makes this more than a toy: model size vs. frame rate,
> FP16 / TensorRT, the latency budget for a car moving at speed. Numbers if you
> have them.

## What stuck with me

> Close on the transferable lesson — for a 4 GB Jetson or a 500-PFLOP cluster,
> the dataset still decides the outcome.
